_comment: One file per procedure_code. Distilled from the matching pair in PM JAY/Standard Treatment Guideline/master/<condition>/ : _Medco.pdf (clinical + mandatory docs) + _PPD.CPD.pdf (PPD/CPD processing checklists). Filename = procedure_code. This is what the Documentation Checker, Query Predictor, and Medical Justification agents read AFTER the picker has chosen the code.
clinical_indications: Spinal hematomas have the potential to cause lasting neurologic damage and disability if not recognized and treated expeditiously.
Manifestation with symptoms of back pain and neurologic deficits, related to the level of the cord affected, although subarachnoid hemorrhage may uniquely manifest with meningitis symptoms as well.
Neurologic deficits are often reversible if diagnosed and treated early with surgical decompression.
Early diagnosis and recognition are essential to prevent progression to paraplegia or quadriplegia.
Patients may present with acute severe back or neck pain.

Objective neurologic deficits vary depending on the location and include several specific manifestations, such as anterior cord syndrome, central cord syndrome, conus medullaris syndrome, and Brown-Séquard syndrome.

preauth.label: Clinical notes with evaluation findings, indication of procedure, and planned line of management

ppd_preauth.q: Clinical notes - detailed history, signs & symptoms, planned line of treatment, indication for procedure?
Did clinical evaluation and imaging confirm the diagnosis?
Was clinical presentation and imaging indicative of surgery?

cpd_claim.q: Are the detailed ICPs with daily vitals and treatment details?
Are the detailed procedure / Operative Notes available?
Was the CT/MRI Spine report indicative of surgery?
Is the Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge?

common_queries: Clinical notes missing detailed history, signs & symptoms, planned line of treatment, or indication for procedure.
Clinical evaluation and/or imaging did not confirm the diagnosis.
Clinical presentation and imaging were not indicative of surgery.
Indication of implant requirement missing at pre-authorization.
Detailed Indoor case papers with daily vitals or treatment details are missing/incomplete.
Detailed procedure / operative notes are missing.
Implant details (invoice/barcode) missing at claim submission.
CT/MRI Spine report was not indicative of surgery.
Discharge summary with follow-up advise at the time of discharge is missing/incomplete.

clinical_key_pointers: Spinal hematomas are an important clinical entity, as they have the potential to cause lasting neurologic damage and disability if they are not recognized and treated expeditiously.
Spinal hematomas are divided into four types, according to their location: Epidural hematomas, Subdural hematomas, Subarachnoid hematomas, Intramedullary hematomas.

Spinal hematomas are often idiopathic; however, other common causes include trauma, iatrogenic diagnostic or pain management interventions, vascular malformations, and coagulopathy or anticoagulant medications.

These conditions may manifest with symptoms of back pain and neurologic deficits, related to the level of the cord affected, although subarachnoid hemorrhage may uniquely manifest with meningitis symptoms as well.

Although smaller hematomas may be managed conservatively, the neurologic deficits are often reversible if diagnosed and treated early with surgical decompression. To allow the best clinical outcome, early diagnosis and recognition are essential for proper conservative or operative treatment and to prevent progression to paraplegia or quadriplegia.
Intramedullary hemorrhage or hematomyelia involves blood products within the substance of the spinal cord, which may occur directly within the cord parenchyma, within a syrinx, or both.
Causes of intramedullary hematoma include: traumatic cord contusion with hemorrhage (most common cause), neoplasm, vascular malformation, coagulopathy, and iatrogenic procedures.

Clinical manifestations of intramedullary hematoma: Patients may present with acute severe back or neck pain. Objective neurologic deficits vary depending on the location and include several specific manifestations, such as anterior cord syndrome, central cord syndrome, conus medullaris syndrome, and Brown-Séquard syndrome.
surgical_options: Although the neurologic status of the patient is paramount to the therapeutic decision, imaging findings are relevant to treatment planning in patients with traumatic intramedullary hemorrhage.
When treatment is necessary, it consists of cervical or thoracic laminectomy followed by durotomy and myelotomy, after which the hematoma cavity is thoroughly evacuated and irrigated.
